items.rationale: His central research program with Lewis-Pye: a formal hierarchy (Perm<QP<DA<FP) showing stronger participation assumptions buy accountability, optimistic responsiveness, and partition-resilience. He's energized that DA-setting impossibilities can be overcome in the QP setting.

citations.quote: all of the impossibility results for the DA setting from the last thread can be overcome in the QP setting
New two-hour tutorial by @AndrewLewisPye on our "Permissionless Consensus" paper

items.rationale: Proves PoS slashing can make attacks expensive (EAAC achievable below 2/3 stake in QP setting), and formalizes the claim that the merge raised Ethereum's economic security. Sees this as foundational to PoS value.

citations.quote: making precise the common belief that the merge has increased Ethereum's economic security
EAAC is achievable by a PoS protocol with slashing, provided the attacker controls less than *two-thirds* of the overall stake

items.rationale: Original EIP-1559 theorist; the core open question (no TFM is simultaneously DSIC, MMIC, OCA-proof) was resolved, and he is actively extending the theory to a post-MEV world with active block producers and searchers.

citations.quote: no transaction fee mechanism can be DSIC, MMIC, and OCA-proof!
Major update to the paper... on transaction fee mechanism design in a post-MEV world

items.rationale: New framework for when validators can be safely reused across services; introduces an overcollateralization factor as an exposable robustness metric. Treats restaking as an important but risk-laden frontier worth rigorous modeling.
items.age_note: Most recent direct mention is the Sept 2024 talk; paper Aug 2024 — within corpus window, not restated in 2025.

citations.quote: New paper with @n_durvasula offers a framework to reason about the risks of restaking.
My work on restaking risks with @n_durvasula now has an accompanying talk

items.rationale: Excited by hard theory becoming the 'fastest zkVM in the world' (Jolt/Succinct) and shares optimism that if SNARKs are practical, even crazier primitives will follow in coming decades.

citations.quote: watching 102 pages of hard theory get turned into the fastest zkVM in the world
if SNARKs can be made practical, *anything* can be made practical

items.rationale: Produced a full tutorial on automated market makers, arbitrage, and loss-versus-rebalancing; treats AMM design and LP economics as a serious analytical domain.
items.age_note: Tutorial videos posted Dec 2024 but reflect EC'24 (summer 2024) work.

citations.quote: Videos from my LVR tutorial with @jason_of_cs @ciamac at EC '24 last summer are up!

items.rationale: Building a theory of PBS and Tullock-contest models for mining/staking/execution tickets/shared sequencing — sees the block-production supply chain as a key design surface.

citations.quote: Toward a theory of proposer-builder separation
Covers mining, staking, execution tickets, shared sequencing

items.rationale: New theory explaining why Dutch auctions are popular in Web3 and which formats resist seller manipulation (shill bids) — connecting classical auction theory to on-chain mechanisms.
items.age_note: Paper April 2024, not restated since; persisted within window.

citations.quote: Why are Dutch auctions so popular in Web3?

items.rationale: His most recent (late-2024/early-2025) public-facing output frames blockchains as a layer enabling genuine ownership of digital goods, contrasting the historical lack of digital property rights.

citations.quote: on (the historical lack of) property rights for what you buy or create in the digital realm
A non-technical 1-hour intro on how blockchain protocols work (from 30,000 feet)

items.rationale: Proves that defending in-protocol requires exotic primitives (VDFs, ephemeral keys); otherwise PoS chains must fall back on out-of-protocol 'social consensus' — a fundamental, unavoidable vulnerability.

citations.quote: fancy primitives like VDFs and ephemeral keys are the *only possible way* for proof-of-stake blockchains to defend against long-range attacks

items.rationale: Shared validators across services make security combinatorial; small shocks (e.g. a slashing bug) can cascade into larger losses, bounded only by overcollateralization buffers.

citations.quote: the maximum-possible length of a cascade of attacks is also governed by the overcollateralization factor
Security is now combinatorial... closely related to the expansion of bipartite graphs.

items.rationale: Shows slashing cannot achieve its purpose in the dynamically-available or partially-synchronous settings — once an adversary can violate consistency it can also evade punishment; withdrawal delays must scale with worst-case message delay.

citations.quote: slashing in a proof-of-stake protocol cannot achieve its intended purpose if message delays cannot be bounded a priori

items.rationale: Any protocol robust to fluctuating participation (Bitcoin-style and PoS longest-chain alike) provably cannot offer accountability, optimistic responsiveness, or partition-resilience — a hard ceiling on that design family.

citations.quote: if your blockchain protocol is robust to fluctuating participation... then it cannot be optimistically responsive, accountable, or robust to network partitions

items.rationale: Repeatedly held up as the well-designed aspirational case: pursuing 'best of both worlds' (longest-chain liveness plus PBFT-style finality) and gaining economic security from the merge. Note: framed as a protocol-design admiration, not an investment call.
items.age_note: Inferred from technical commentary; he never expresses a price/investment view.

citations.quote: Ethereum is effectively aspiring toward the best of both worlds
the merge has increased Ethereum's economic security

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden is a theorist; the entire corpus is research on consensus, mechanism design, and cryptoeconomic security. He makes no statements about market direction, asset allocation, valuations, or risk appetite. No risk-on/risk-off signal can be honestly extracted.

notes: Tim Roughgarden's public statements are almost exclusively academic — permissionless-consensus theory, EIP-1559/TFM design, MEV/PBS, restaking risk, slashing/EAAC, AMM/LVR, auction theory, and SNARK/zkVM progress. 'Sectors' here map his research enthusiasm and concern, not portfolio views. He names blockchains (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Algorand, Chia, Avalanche) only as technical exemplars; the lone token-level lean (Ethereum, admiring its design and merge-driven economic security) is extrapolated and low-conviction, not a market call. He expresses essentially zero view on tokens as investments or on market risk regime. His clearest 'concerns' are impossibility results — fundamental limits (long-range attacks needing exotic crypto, restaking cascades, slashing limits under weak assumptions, longest-chain trade-offs) rather than market risks. Most-recent output (Dec 2024–Mar 2025) shifted toward public-education content on blockchains and digital ownership; no stance reversals detected across the 18-month span — his theoretical positions accumulate rather than conflict.
